1.blurb: The Germanic peoples — Goths, Vandals, Franks, Saxons — had been Rome's restless northern neighbors for four centuries before the Huns sent them crashing through the frontier. The centuries of low-level raiding, treaty-making, and federate service that built up the skills and relationships the migrations would exploit are the Germanic story in full.

The Goths were the first domino in the whole chain, the people the Huns hit first and who broke into the empire in 376. Their own story — from the Pontic steppe through the Adrianople catastrophe, the sack of Rome in 410, and the long migration to a kingdom in Spain — is a book in itself, running parallel to everything in these chapters.

The Mercenary War of 241–238 BCE, when Carthage armed foreign soldiers it could not pay and nearly got destroyed from the inside, was seven centuries old by the time Rome learned the same lesson with the foederati. Two sea-power civilizations, centuries apart, both undermined by the soldiers they settled on their own soil.

The Roman Empire the Huns set in motion in 375 had been building for a thousand years before its western half dissolved. The arc from the Republic's legions to the federate system that gave the empire away is the long arc of the Roman story — the context that makes the migration period's speed of dissolution comprehensible.

2.blurb: The Vandals, Suebi, and Franks who crossed the Rhine on the last night of 406 had been pressing against that border for generations, shaped by centuries of contact with Rome — trading, raiding, serving in the legions. The Rhine crossing was not the first act of the Germanic story, only its most consequential one.

While the Vandals and Alans were flooding into Gaul, the Visigoths were already inside the empire — dealing with the aftermath of Adrianople, following Alaric through Italy, about to sack Rome in 410. The two migrations ran in parallel, the Gothic story threading through the same years as the Rhine crossing's consequences played out.

Britain, the first whole province to fall off the map in 410, did not stay empty. Within a generation of the Roman withdrawal, Angles, Saxons, and Jutes were crossing the North Sea into a province whose Roman institutions had already collapsed. What they built on top of the rubble, in a landscape with no surviving Roman state to inherit, is a genuinely different story from the continental successor kingdoms.

3.blurb: The Visigothic kingdom of Toulouse, the prototype successor state, was the Goths' reward for a generation of chaos inside the empire — Adrianople, the sack of Rome, a long march through Spain and back. The settlement in Aquitaine in 418 is the hinge between the Gothic story and the wider story of barbarian kingdoms replacing Roman provinces.

The federate system that turned barbarian war-bands into kingdoms had its roots in the centuries of Roman management of Germanic peoples along the Rhine and Danube — small defeated groups planted as farmer-soldiers in emptied frontier land. The 420s and 430s were the moment that long-running experiment escaped Roman control entirely.

4.blurb: Six hundred years before Geiseric, Carthage had been exactly what the Vandal kingdom became — a sea power sitting at the center of the western Mediterranean, throttling Rome's grain supply and forcing it to fight for its survival. Contemporaries noticed the grim symmetry: a sea-power rival had risen again at Carthage, in the ruins of the one Rome had destroyed.

While Geiseric was building his Vandal kingdom in Africa in the 430s and 440s, the Visigoths were consolidating in Aquitaine and beginning to expand into Spain. The two greatest successor kingdoms of the western Mediterranean were taking shape simultaneously — one controlling the sea, one holding the land bridge to the Pyrenees.

5.blurb: The Visigoths were the decisive contingent at the Catalaunian Plains — Theodoric I's death in the battle became a Visigothic founding legend. The coalition Aetius assembled against Attila was only possible because the barbarian kingdoms Rome had spent fifty years creating had interests of their own, chief among them not being absorbed into Attila's confederation.

Attila's campaigns against Constantinople and then the West happened against the backdrop of an Eastern Empire that had never stopped functioning — still wealthy enough to pay the tribute, still capable of fielding armies on two fronts. The contrast with the hollowed Western state is the deepest structural fact of the period.

6.blurb: As the Western Empire's last emperors cycled in and out, the Visigoths in Aquitaine were building the most stable polity in the former western provinces. Ricimer made and unmade emperors in Ravenna; the Visigoths were quietly becoming a real kingdom in Toulouse, with their own law, their own church, and increasingly their own ambitions beyond the deal Rome had given them.

The Western emperors between 455 and 476 were figureheads for Germanic generals while the Eastern Empire, still governed from Constantinople, watched the process from a distance and recognized some puppets as legitimate while refusing others. The Roman Empire did not end in 476 — half of it kept going for another thousand years.

When Odoacer sent the imperial regalia back to Constantinople in 476, he was acknowledging what the East had always claimed — that there was one Roman Empire, governed from the Bosphorus, and the West was temporarily under barbarian management. The Eastern Empire that received those regalia would eventually try to make that claim militarily real.

7.blurb: Theodoric the Great's Ostrogothic Italy and the Visigothic kingdom in Spain were the two most Roman-looking successor states — both Arian, both employing Roman administrators, both preserving Roman legal culture for their Roman subjects. When Clovis drove the Visigoths out of Gaul at Vouille in 507, the Gothic story shifted entirely to Spain and Italy.

The Frankish kingdom Clovis built — Catholic, Roman-administered, allied with the bishops — is the seed of everything that comes next in western Europe. The alliance of the Merovingian crown with the Catholic Church planted in the 490s and 500s is exactly where early medieval Europe begins, handing forward to Charlemagne and the world of the eighth and ninth centuries.

The successor kings who wrote down law — Lex Salica, Breviary of Alaric, Gundobad's dual codes — were doing so in Latin, using Roman legal categories, explicitly claiming to be Rome's heirs and managers. The Roman legal tradition they preserved became the foundation of continental European law, a thread running unbroken from the Digest of Justinian to modern civil-law systems.

8.blurb: Justinian's reconquest was only conceivable because the Eastern Empire had never stopped being a functioning state — still collecting taxes, still fielding professional armies, still building monumental architecture like the Hagia Sophia at the same time it was funding campaigns in Africa and Italy. The contrast with the hollow Western shell it was trying to restore is the whole story of the reconquest.

The Gothic War in Italy that ground on from 535 to 554 was the Ostrogoths' last chapter — the final act of the story that began when the Huns pushed the Goths across the Danube in 376. King Totila's campaigns, the sieges of Rome, the final defeat under Narses — the full Gothic narrative belongs to the Gothic book.

9.blurb: The Plague of Justinian that wrecked the Eastern Empire's ability to hold its western reconquests in the 540s and 550s was the same pandemic that hit Constantinople in 541 and returned in waves for two centuries. The Eastern Empire's long-term demographic and fiscal decline, the context for its eventual contraction, begins with the same bacterium that ended Justinian's western ambitions.

The Lombard conquest of 568 created the political patchwork — Lombard duchies, Byzantine exarchates, and a papacy governing Rome as a de facto city-state — that is the opening condition of early medieval Italy. The fragmentation the Lombards made permanent is exactly where the early medieval story in the West picks up.

While the Lombards were walking into a ruined Italy in 568, the Angles, Saxons, and Jutes had been building something genuinely new in Britain for over a century — a post-Roman world that did not inherit the Latin church, the Roman law, or the surviving elite that shaped the continental successor kingdoms. Two different ways of being post-Roman, happening at the same time on opposite ends of the former empire.
